text: There are only few clinical data on non-oncological procedures performed with the new HUGO robot-assisted surgery system. We describe step-by-step two different surgical techniques for RASP performed by two experienced robotic surgeons. We also report the first large series of RASP performed with this new robotic platform.

The surgeon performs the procedure using an anterior transperitoneal approach, incising the peritoneum at the intersection of the medial umbilical ligament and the vas deferens. The paravesical space is exposed until the endopelvic fascia is visible. The urachus is coagulated and cut, followed by a longitudinal cystostomy at the prostate-vesicle junction. A stitch is placed in the middle lobe for traction. The bilateral ureteric orifices are identified to prevent injury. Sharp and blunt dissection develops the plane between the prostatic adenoma and pseudocapsule, starting at the six o'clock position on the right lobe, then the left lobe. The prostatic urethra is transected at the apex, and the prostatic adenoma is fully dissected. Hemostasis is achieved, and the prostatic fossa is retrigonized with a single-layer running suture from the urethra to the bladder neck. The cystotomy is closed with a double-layer running suture, and a three-way catheter is placed for continuous irrigation.

The surgeon performs the procedure using a posterior approach, starting with a posterior midline cystostomy. The bladder is retracted using three exovulporal sutures, and the bilateral ureteric orifices are identified to prevent injury. The bladder neck is incised at the six o'clock position to expose the prostatic adenoma. Sharp and blunt dissection is used to develop the plane between the prostatic adenoma and the pseudocapsule circumferentially on both sides. After complete dissection, the prostatic fossa is inspected for hemostasis, and single stitches are placed to prevent bleeding. A three-way catheter with continuous irrigation is inserted, and the cystostomy incision is closed with single stitches.

Twenty patients underwent RASP at OLV hospital between February, and March, Fifty-five percent of the patients had an episode of acute urinary retention and forty percent of them had in the willing blood catheter. Four patients had a concomitant bladder stone and two a bladder diverticulum. The median operative time was one hundred and sixty-five minutes. The median console time was one hundred and twenty-five minutes. No intraoperative complication was recorded, no drainage was placed, and eighteen percent of the patients removed the catheter on the first postoperative day. On final pathology, the median prostate volume was one hundred and twenty grams. On postoperative uroflometry, the median Qmax was sixteen, the post volume residual fifteen. These series represent the first worldwide large series of RASP executed with the novel Hugo-RAS system. These preliminary results are comparable with the previous robotic system, suggesting the multiple potential use of Hugo-RAS.
